source.title: NPNF-211. Sulpitius Severus, Vincent of Lerins, John Cassian

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: “But in what I am now about to narrate, I possess you, Sulpitius” (here he looked at me) “as a fellow-witness.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: One day, I and Sulpitius there were watching before Martin’s door, and had already sat in silence for several hours.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: We did so with deep reverence and awe, as if we were carrying out a watch prescribed to us before the tent of an angel; while, all the time, the door of his cell being closed, he did not know that we were there.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Meanwhile, we heard the sound of people conversing, and by and by we were filled with a kind of awe and amazement, for we could not help perceiving that something divine was going on.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: After nearly two hours, Martin comes out to us; and then our friend Sulpitius (for no one was accustomed to speak to him more familiarly) began to entreat him to make known to us, piously enquiring on the subject, what meant that sort of Divine awe which we confessed we had both felt, and with whom he had been conversing in his cell.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: We added that, as we stood before the door, we had undoubtedly heard a feeble sound of people talking, but had scarcely understood it.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Then he after a long delay (but there was really nothing which Sulpitius could not extort from him even against his will: I am about to relate things somewhat difficult of belief, but, as Christ is my witness, I lie not, unless any one is so impious as to think that Martin himself lied) said: ‘I will tell you, but I beg you will not speak of it to any one else.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Agnes, Thecla, and Mary were there with me.’

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: He proceeded to describe to us the face and general aspect of each.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: And he acknowledged that, not merely on that day, but frequently, he received visits from them.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Nor did he deny that Peter also and Paul, the Apostles, were pretty frequently seen by him.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Moreover, he was in the habit of rebuking the demons by their special names, according as they severally came to him.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: He found Mercury a cause of special annoyance, while he said that Jupiter was stupid and doltish.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: I am aware that these things seemed incredible even to many who dwelt in the same monastery; and far less can I expect that all who simply hear of them will believe them.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: For unless Martin had lived such an inestimable life, and displayed such excellence, he would by no means be regarded among us as having been endowed with so great glory.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: And yet it is not at all wonderful that human infirmity doubted concerning the works of Martin, when we see that many at the present day do not even believe the Gospels.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: But we have ourselves had personal knowledge and experience, that angels often appeared and spoke familiarly with Martin.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: As bearing upon this, I am to narrate a matter, of small importance indeed, but still I will state it.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: A synod, composed of bishops, was held at Nemausus, and while he had refused to attend it, he was nevertheless desirous of knowing what was done at it.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: It so happened that our friend Sulpitius was then on board ship with him, but, as was his custom, he kept his place at a distance from the rest, in a retired part of the vessel.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: There an angel announced to him what had taken place in the synod.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: And when, afterwards, we carefully enquired into the time at which the council was held, we found, beyond all doubt, that that was the very day of the council, and that those things were there decreed by the bishops which the angel had announced to Martin.
